Abstract 27

- Diversification  of  the  hexaploid  (bread)  wheat  genetic  pool  using  wild  genetic  resources  relies  on 28
- effective meiotic recombination (crossover) between wheat chromosomes and their counterparts from 29
- related species (homoeologues). However, crossover between homoeologues is normally suppressed 30
- by two major genes, ZIP4-5B ( Ph1 ) and MSH7-3D ( Ph2 ). We investigated the effect of introducing 31
- zip4-5B and/or msh7-3D mutations  into  interspecific  hybrids  derived  from  crosses  between  wheat 32
- and Aegilops  variabilis .  Single  and  double  mutants  were  exploited  in  Chinese  Spring  (CS)  and 33
- Cadenza (Cad) genetic backgrounds, as well as in a CS/Cad recombinant background. The number of 34
- univalents,  bivalents  and  multivalents  was  scored  from  meiotic  cells  at  metaphase  I,  from  which 35
- numbers of chiasmata were deduced. We demonstrated a non-cumulative effect of simultaneous zip436
- 5B and msh7-3D mutations on homoeologous recombination, as homoeologous crossovers reached a 37
- maximum when ZIP4-5B alone was mutated. We also showed that hybrids carrying both the zip4-5B 38
- and msh7-3D mutations  in  the  same  genetic  background  exhibited  more  effective  recombination 39
- compared to a double mutant in the CS/Cad recombinant background. The progression of meiosis 40
- was also monitored in the different interspecific hybrids mutants, revealing clear disruptions. Thus, 41
- our study significantly contributes to the optimization of the introgression of beneficial alleles from 42
- wild  relatives  into  elite  wheat  germplasm;  first  by  demonstrating  the  efficiency  of ZIP4-5B and 43
- MSH7-3D mutations independently and in combination and second by elucidating the influence of 44

45

- the genetic background in which these mutations are present in an interspecific hybrid context.

1 Introduction

Bread wheat ( Triticum aestivum L.) accounts for 20% of our global calorie consumption (Reynolds et al.,  2012),  yet  concerns  have  been  raised  that  the  current  rate  of  yield  improvement  may  be insufficient to meet future demand, with projections indicating that at least a 40% increase will be required by 2050 (Charmet, 2011; Le Gouis et al., 2020; Ray et al., 2013).

Presently, our capacity to accelerate the rate of genetic improvement in wheat is constrained by (1) changes  in  agricultural  practices  for  economic  or  regulatory  reasons,  such  as  the  need  to  reduce mineral  nitrogen  fertilizer  application,  or  measures  to  improve  agricultural  sustainability  such  as reductions in pesticide usage (Peltonen-Sainio et al., 2015); (2) climate change, which is becoming increasingly important and threatens yield potential as well as the stability of yield (Brisson et al., 2010; Le Gouis et al., 2020; Porter and Semenov, 2005; Tester and Langridge, 2010); (3) slowing of genetic gains due to environmental issues such as drought stress (Manès et al., 2012; Touzy et al., 2019).  This  suggests  that  new  wheat  varieties  must  be  more  tolerant  to  environmental  stresses, especially drought and heat, and less dependent on fertilizers, insecticides or fungicides.

One way to face this challenge is to tap into wheat wild relatives, which serve as a large reservoir of unexploited alleles/genes, the value of which has been demonstrated in the genetic improvement of many agronomically important traits (Feuillet et al., 2008; King et al., 2024; Marcussen et al., 2014). Actually, it has been demonstrated that the introgression of genetic material from wild relatives has occurred during the evolution of wheat without the intervention of man (Cheng et al., 2019). From the  1970s  onward,  there  has  been  a  concerted  effort  by  wheat  researchers  to  hybridize  such  wild species with domesticated wheat with the aim of introducing novel beneficial diversity (Laugerotte et al.,  2022).  This  resulted  in  a  reshuffling  of  the  wheat  genome,  and  produced  numerous  structural variants  larger  than  5  Mb  in  most  modern  cultivars  (Balfourier  et  al.,  2019).  Some  of  these  wild relative  introgressions  have  been  widely  used  in  wheat  breeding  programs  like  the  1RS/1BL wheat/rye  ( Secale cereale )  translocation  (Cai  and  Liu,  1989)  and  the  2A/2N  introgression  derived from Aegilops ventricosa (Helguera et al., 2003).

The main challenge in wheat wild relative introgression remains the exchange between wheat and 87 wild relative chromosomes. Such exchanges rely on meiotic recombination, a process common to all 88 sexually reproducing eukaryotes, and mandatory for viable and fertile haploid gamete production (for 89 review,  see  Mercier  et  al.,  2015).  Meiotic  recombination,  also  called  crossover  (CO)  is  tightly 90 controlled, both in the number and location of recombination events (reviewed in Girard et al., 2023). 91 Moreover,  because  bread  wheat  is  an  allopolyploid  species  (genome  AABBDD;  2n  =  6x  =  42), 92 deriving  from  two  natural  interspecific  hybridizations  of  three  closely  related  diploid  species  ( T. 93 urartu (AA), Ae. speltoides (SS related to BB), and T. tauschii (DD); Marcussen et al., 2014), there is 94 an additional layer of control preventing COs between related homoeologous chromosomes within 95 the wheat genome (Mason and Wendel, 2020; Sourdille et al., 2025). 96

- In bread wheat, early genetic and cytogenetic studies revealed the presence of at least two loci, Ph1 97
- and Ph2 ,  located  on  chromosome-arms  5BL  and  3DS  respectively,  controlling  recombination 98
- between  its  homoeologues  (Mello-Sampayo,  1971;  Mello-Sampayo  and  Lorente,  1968;  Riley  and 99
- Chapman, 1958; Sears and Okamoto, 1958). A large deletion encompassing Ph1 was developed by 100
- Sears  in  Chinese  Spring  (CSph1b,  1977),  covering  nearly  60Mb  and  ~1200  genes  (Martín  et  al., 101
- 102
- 2018). However, the line carrying this deletion has accumulated extensive rearrangements over the
- years, including additional deletions, chromosome exchanges and duplications (Martín et al., 2018), 103
- thereby  reducing  the  efficiency  of  the  introgression  process  during  breeding.  Previous  studies 104

- delimited the Ph1 locus to a region containing a cluster of CDK2-like genes, as well as a block of 105
- heterochromatin duplicated from chromosome 3B (Al-Kaff et al., 2008; Griffiths et al., 2006; Martín 106
- et  al.,  2017).  This  heterochromatin  block  contained  a  copy  of  ZIP4,  a  protein  required  for  the 107
- formation  of  type-I  COs  (Chelysheva  et  al.,  2007).  More  recently,  exploitation  of  Ethyl-Methyl 108
- Sulfonate (EMS), as well as CRISPR-Cas9 mutants of this additional-copy of ZIP4 on 5B ( zip4-5B , 109
- originally named TaZip4-B2 ; Rey et al., 2017), showed that zip4-5B mutants mimic the original ph1 110
- Sears' phenotype, exhibiting a significantly increased number of homoeologous COs (Alabdullah et 111
- al.,  2021;  Martín  et  al., 2021; Rey  et  al., 2018,  2017).  These  mutants  revealed  that  this  additional 112
- ZIP4-5B copy is also required for complete synapsis of homologues, with loss of this copy leading to 113
- incomplete synapsis (Draeger et al., 2023). Although the exact mode of action of ZIP4-5B remains 114
- unclear, evidence from tetraploid and hexaploid wheat suggests that it promotes early homologous 115

116

- synapsis, thereby reducing the opportunity for homoeologues to synapse and then crossover later in

- zip4-5B mutants  exhibit  distinct  meiotic  behaviours,  with  some  retaining  a  high  level  of  fertility
- despite facilitating homoeologous recombination, demonstrating a dual function for ZIP4-5B (Martín et al., 2021, 2014).

As regards to Ph2

, two mutants were initially produced in the reference cultivar Chinese Spring: an

- irradiation mutant ( ph2a ; Sutton et al., 2003), with a deletion covering ~125 Mb of the distal part of
- the  short  arm  of  chromosome  3D  (Sva č ina  et  al.,  2020),  and  an  EMS  mutant  ( ph2b ;  Wall  et  al.,
- 1971).  Previous  attempts  to  clone Ph2 suggested  several  different  genes  as  potential  candidates,

including WM1 (Ji and Langridge, 1994; Whitford, 2002), WM3 (Letarte, 1996), WM5 (Dong et al.,

- 2005) and TaMsh7 (Dong et al.,  2002;  Lloyd  et  al.,  2007).  Recently, MSH7-3D (originally  named
2. TaMSH7-D1 ) was found to correspond to Ph2 (Serra et al., 2021), coding for a plant specific MSH7

protein (Mut-S homolog 7) belonging to the DNA Mismatch Repair (MMR) complex (Culligan and

- Hays,  2000).  Interestingly,  MSH7  has  also  been  found  to  be  a  key  factor  in  homoeologous
- recombination in tomato ( Solanum lycopersicum ;  Tam et al., 2011). It has been suggested that the
- MSH7-3D protein  could  play  a  crucial  role  during  the  early  steps  of  recombination,  by  rejecting
- heteroduplex single-strand invasion between homoeologues (Serra et al., 2021).
- Hybrids between wild relatives and wheat can be used to score homoeologous chiasmata (the visible
- manifestation  of  crossover  events),  as  there  are  no  homologous  chromosomes  present  in  such
- hybrids, only homoeologues. Previous studies utilized such hybrids to assess the interaction between
- Ph1 and Ph2 on  homoeologous  CO.  Mello-Sampayo  and  Canas  (1973)  assessed  homoeologous
- chiasmata in crosses involving aneuploid wheats, missing either full chromosomes (3A, 3D or 5B) or
- chromosome-arms (3AS, 3BS, 3DS and 3AS + 3DS), and Aegilops sharonensis (genome SshSsh) or
- rye ( Secale cereale ; genome RR). They observed that the number of homoeologous chiasmata was
- greatest  when  chromosome  5B  was  missing,  compared  to  when  either  chromosome  3D  or
- chromosome-arm 3DS was missing. In the absence of chromosome 5B, 50% of the 28 homoeologous 141

142

- chromosomes were linked by chiasmata on both their chromosome arms. The additional loss of 3DS

143

- did not increase chiasma formation; instead, it caused a slight reduction in the average number of
- chiasmata per cell (15.07 vs 13.84, respectively). 144

145

- Ceoloni and Donini (1993) applied a similar approach, exploiting hybrids with Aegilops variabilis

146

147

148

149

150

- (UUSvSv; 2n = 28) or rye to evaluate meiotic chromosome configurations, with the aim of deducing
- the extent of homoeologous chiasmata. Contrary to Mello-Sampayo and Canas (1973), they observed
- an 8% higher level of homoeologous chiasmata when both Ph1 and Ph2 were absent, compared to
- when  just Ph1 was  absent.  This  difference  was  due  to  an  increase  in  chiasmata  linking  multiple
- chromosomes.  However,  they  highlighted  the  difficulty  of  rapidly  and  unambiguously  screening

- homozygous and heterozygous  double ph1/ph2 mutants,  as  meiosis-based  checks  were  sometimes 151

152

unreliable due to background effects from large deletions.

- In this study we took advantage of the recent cloning of both Ph1 and Ph2 (Rey et al., 2017; Serra et 153
- al., 2021), to generate a large and comprehensive set of crosses involving these two loci and to design 154
- specific  markers  for  each  locus.  Using  these  markers,  we  screened  crosses  between zip4-5B ( ph1 ) 155
- and/or msh7-3D ( ph2 )  mutants and Ae. variabilis ,  to  identify  specific  single and double mutations. 156
- This approach enabled us to assess the individual and combined contributions of ZIP4-5B and MSH7157
- 3D to  homoeologous  CO  formation,  focusing  on  their  specific  effects  rather  than  the  broader 158
- consequences of deletions or loss of whole chromosomes covering multiple genes. 159

2 Materials & Methods 160

2.1 Plant material 161

- WheatAegilops variabilis interspecific hybrids were produced using five different wheat mutants in 162
- two genetic backgrounds: Chinese Spring (CS) and Cadenza (Cad). For CS, ph1b (deletion of 59.3 163
- Mb; (Sears, 1977) and ph2b (EMS mutant; Wall et al., 1971) reference mutants were used. For Cad, 164
- three EMS  mutants from the John Innes Centre collection (https://www.jic.ac.uk/research165
- impact/germplasm-resource-unit/; Krasileva et al., 2017) were used: two zip4-5B mutants (Cad0348 166
- and Cad1691; (Martín et al., 2021; Rey et al., 2017) and one msh7-3D mutant (Cad2006; Serra et al., 167
- 2021). Wild-type accessions for CS and Cad were used as control. 168
- The crossing strategy used to generate the mutant collection is illustrated in Figure 1. Within each 169
- genetic  background  (CS  and  Cad),  single  wheat  mutants  were  first  inter-crossed  to  generate 170
- ph1b/ph2b double mutants in CS or zip4-5B/msh7-3D double mutants in Cad (Fig. 1A a,b). These 171
- double mutants were then crossed between backgrounds to produce CS x Cad double mutants (Fig 172
- 1A c). Single ph1 and ph2 mutants from the two different genetic backgrounds were also crossed 173
- (Fig.  1A d,e).  Unfortunately, the cross between the ph1 mutants was not successful, and we  were 174
- therefore unable to obtain the CS ph1b /Cad zip4-5B genotype (Fig. 1A e). In parallel, the CS and Cad 175
- wildtypes were also crossed (Fig 1A f). The resulting hybrids (CS/Cad (f), CS ph2b /Cad msh7-3D 176
- (d), CS ph1b Cad zip4-5B / CS ph2b Cad msh7-3D (c)) were finally crossed with Aegilops variabilis 177
- (Aev;  ERGE  code  26248)  to  generate  interspecific  haploid  hybrids  (Fig.  1B).  Twenty-nine 178
- interspecific  haploid  hybrids  were  obtained  following  this  scheme  and  evaluated  (Supplementary 179
- Table 1). Molecular analyses were conducted to check their mutant status: wildtype, CS ph2b or Cad 180
- msh7-3D single  mutant;  CS ph1b/ph2b or  Cad zip4-5B/msh7-3D double  mutant;  or  CS/Cad 181
- recombined  double  mutant  (CS ph1b /Cad msh7-3D or  Cad zip4-5B /CS ph2b ).  After  analysis,  the 182

2.2 Molecular analyses 222

- DNA was isolated from 100 mg of fresh leaves using a magnetic beads protocol (Sbeadex mini plant 223
- kit,  LGC,  Teddington,  United  Kingdom),  with  concentrations  adjusted  to  10ng/µL.  PCR  was 224
- performed in 11 µL (5 µL Master Mix AmpliTaq Gold™ 360, 1 µL Master Mix 360 GC Enhancer, 225
- 0.5 µL primers mix (forward and reverse at 10 µM, 1.5 µL water, 3 µL of DNA at 10ng/µL) under 226
- the following conditions: initial denaturation at 95°C for 10 min; 35 cycles of denaturation at 95°C 227
- for 30 s, annealing at Tm for 30 s, and elongation at 72°C for 50 s; followed by a final extension at 228
- 72°C  for  10  min.  Sequences  of  primers  and  Tm  are  given  in  Supplementary  Table  3.  The 229
- presence/absence of Ph1 and/or Ph2 mutations were scored on 1.2% agarose gel prepared in TAE 1X 230
- (Tris 0.04 M, Acetate 0.04 M, EDTA 0.001 M) and run during 30 minutes at 100V. 231

2.3 Cytogenetic studies and statistics 232

- Anther extraction, meiocyte preparation and microscopy observations were as described in (Bazile et 233
- al.,  2024).  Briefly,  tillers  with  immature  inflorescences  were  collected  in  the  morning  and  placed 234
- immediately on ice. Spikes were carefully removed from the sheath, and the three anthers of each 235
- flower extracted. One anther was stained with Acetocarmine (10 g/L Carmin, 45% acetic acid) and 236
- observed under a microscope to check for the meiotic stage. The remaining two synchronized anthers 237
- were fixed in Carnoy solution (EtOH 100% - glacial acetic acid; v/v 3:1) for 48 hours, followed by 238
- storage in EtOH 70% at 4°C. To analyse meiotic progression of different hybrids from zygotene to 239
- the tetrad stage in detail, one anther from each stage was placed on a poly-L-lysine-coated slide with 240
- a drop of acetocarmine and opened using two roll pins under binocular to expose the meiocytes. A 241
- drop of 45% acetic acid was applied to remove acetocarmine, and slides were immersed in liquid 242
- nitrogen,  air-dried  briefly,  and  mounted  with  Vectashield-DAPI  (Eurobio-Ingen).  Images  were 243

244

captured  using  an  Axio  Observer  Z1  fluorescence  microscope  with  Zen  software  (Carl  Zeiss

- microscopy). 245
- The number of cells examined per genotype ranged from 48 to 139 (Table 1). Numbers of univalents, 246
- bivalents (ring and rod) and multivalents (including complex pairing configurations) were counted 247
- manually, and numbers of chiasmata deduced from these counts, according to the method described 248
- in  Supplementary  Figure  1.  For  each  dataset,  normality  of  distribution  was  checked  using  the 249
- normality  test  of  Shapiro  and  Wilk  with  default  values.  Since  the  data  did  not  follow  a  normal 250
- distribution, a non-parametric Mann-Whitney test was applied. 251

3 Results 258

- In  total,  33  interspecific  wheatAe.  variabilis allo-haploid  hybrids  were  generated,  using  wheat 259

260

mutants in Chinese Spring (CS) or Cadenza (Cad) backgrounds affecting the Ph1

and/or Ph2

loci,

- together  with  their  corresponding  wild-type  controls  (see  Materials  and  Methods;  Supplementary 261
- Table 2). All plants showed normal development, with the exception of plant 13D (a CS ph1b/ph2b 262
- double mutant derived interspecific hybrid), which exhibited a slight delay in flowering compared to 263
- the others (Supplementary Figure 2). Three spikes from each hybrid were bagged to check for self264
- fertility. As expected, no seed was produced, confirming the sterility of the allo-haploid interspecific 265
- hybrids. Given the genomic constitution of bread wheat (AABBDD; 2n = 6x = 42; n = 21) and Ae. 266
- variabilis (UUSvSv;  2n  =  4x  =  28;  n  =  14),  interspecific  hybrid  meiocytes  must  contain  35 267 chromosomes. 268

3.1 Analysis of interspecific hybrids with ph1 or ph2 mutations at meiotic metaphase I 269

- Numbers  of  univalents,  bivalents  (ring  and  rod)  and  multivalents  (more  than  two  chromosomes 270 linked by chiasmata) at metaphase I stage were scored for each interspecific hybrid, and total number 271 of homoeologous chiasmata was subsequently deduced (Table 1; Figure 2). 272
- Hybrids derived from crosses involving wild-type plants gave similar values for CS and Cad, with 273

4 Discussion 367

- For nearly 40 years, wheat breeders have used the Sears mutant, carrying a deletion of the Ph1 locus, 368
- to introgress agronomically useful genes or alleles (homoeologous chromosomes) from wild relatives 369
- into  wheat  (King  et  al.,  2019).  Recently,  researchers  revealed  that  the  effect  of  the Ph1 locus  in 370
- tetraploid and hexaploid wheat (consisting of both homologous and homoeologous chromosomes) is 371
- explained by a dual function ZIP4-5B gene,  coding  for  a  protein  required  in  type-I  CO  formation 372
- (Chelysheva et al., 2007). This promotes the synapsis of homologous chromosomes during meiosis, 373
- whilst  also  suppressing  crossover  between  the  homoeologous  chromosomes  (Draeger  et  al.,  2023; 374
- Martín et al., 2021, 2017; Rey et al., 2017). 375
- As regards to Ph2 , this locus has also been recognised as playing a role in CO during wheat meiosis, 376
- its function has only recently been attributed to the MSH7-3D gene (Serra et al., 2021), coding for the 377
- plant specific MSH7 protein ( Mut-S homolog 7 ). This protein belongs to the DNA Mismatch Repair 378
- (MMR) complex (Culligan and Hays, 2000). It has been suggested that this MSH7-3D protein could 379
- play a crucial role during the early steps of recombination, by rejecting heteroduplex single-strand 380
- invasion between homoeologues (Serra et al., 2021). 381
- Our  work  investigated  whether  the  wheat  genetic  background  and  the  combinations  of  different 382
- mutations  in  these  two  genes  could  enhance  the  efficiency  of  crossover  between  homoeologous 383
- chromosomes in hybrids of wheat and a wild relative.  In our study,  hybrids derived  from  crosses 384
- between Ae. variabilis (Aev) and Chinese Spring (CS) ph1b and/or ph2b mutants developed in the 385
- 1970s,  were  compared  to  hybrids  derived  from  crosses  using  the  recent  Cadenza  (Cad) zip4-5B 386
- and/or msh7-3D . 387

388

389

4.1 Wheat genetic background and/or mutation type in Ph1 and Ph2 affects the level of homoeologous recombination

- In interspecific hybrids derived from crosses between different bread wheat (CS and Cad) and the 390
- wild tetraploid species Aev, we demonstrated that CS and Cad wild types gave similar results, both 391
- displaying  similarly  high  numbers  of  univalents  and  very  low  chiasma  frequencies,  indicating  a 392
- comparable suppression of homoeologous CO in both wheat backgrounds. Additionally, we showed 393
- that ph1 and ph2 mutations result in a global increase of rod and ring bivalents and multivalents at 394
- the  expense  of  univalents,  leading  to  an  increased  number  of  chiasmata  between  homoeologous 395
- chromosomes. We also observed that the effects of ph1 mutations alone are greater than those of ph2 , 396
- regardless of whether the background is Cad or CS. For example, in terms of chiasma number, the 397
- effect of the ph1 mutation was approximately two-fold higher than the msh7-3D mutation in a Cad 398
- background, and about 1.4 times higher than the ph2b mutation in a CS background. 399
- All these findings are in line with previous studies (Table 2; Bochev et al., 1978; Ceoloni and Donini, 400
- 1993; Rey et al., 2017; Serra et al., 2021). 401

Interestingly, differences were observed between Cad zip4-5B and CS ph1b mutants. Although both 405 genotypes displayed a similar number of rod bivalents, Cad zip4-5B formed a higher number of ring 406 bivalents, whereas CS ph1b showed a greater frequency of multivalents. This suggests that Cad zip4407 5B supports a more regular pairing behaviour. This could be explained by the fact that CS ph1b has 408 accumulated rearrangements during its propagation, which will lead to the background occurrence of 409 multivalents  (Martín  et  al.,  2018).  However,  the  difference  between  the  two  mutants  is  not  large, 410 suggesting that the EMS mutation of zip4-5B in  Cadenza mimics well the effect of the deletion of 411 Ph1 in Chinese Spring. 412

- We  also  observed  consistent  values  for  the  Cad msh7-3D mutant  compared  to  previous  studies. 413
- However, in our study, the CS ph2b mutant exhibited more bivalents and multivalents, and therefore 414
- more chiasmata, compared to the Cad msh7-3D mutant. It is possible, as in the case of the CS ph1b 415
- mutant, that the forty-year-old CS ph2b mutant also carries background chromosome rearrangements, 416
- affecting  its  meiotic  configurations  in  hybrids.  Furthermore,  our  study  highlights  that  the  Chinese 417
- Spring  double  mutant has a  higher  level  of  CO  than  the  Cadenza  double  mutant.  This  may  again 418
- reflect the relative level of background rearrangements in the older CS ph1b and CS ph2b compared 419
- to the very recent Cad zip4-5B and Cad msh7-3D mutants. 420

4.2 Accumulation of ph1 and ph2 mutations does not improve homoeologous recombination 421

- We observed no further increase in chiasmata number in the double mutants compared to the ph1b or 422
- zip4-5B single mutants alone; in some cases, chiasma number was slightly lower. This indicates that 423
- when both zip4-5B and msh7-3D are  mutated/eliminated, homoeologous CO reaches its maximum 424
- with the ph1 mutation alone, as Mello-Sampayo and Canas (1973) reported in the context of CS x Ae. 425
- sharonensis . This evidence suggests that the two genes may be acting within the same pathway (type 426

- I  CO),  albeit  at  different  stages  of  the  process  (Sourdille  et  al.,  2025).  Right  after  double-strand 427
- breaks  are  produced  in  early  prophase  I,  it  is  likely  that  their  repair  can  be  initiated  on  both 428
- homologues or homoeologues that are very close because of the bouquet structure (Martinez et al., 429
- 2001; Osman et al., 2011). However, the mismatch repair protein complex TaMSH2/TaMSH7 ( Ph2 ) 430
- certainly detects the numerous differences between the homoeologues and contributes to prevent the 431
- single-strand invasion and the initiation of homoeologous synapsis (Serra et al., 2021). In the same 432
- time, TaZip4-B2 ( Ph1 ) promotes homologous synapsis during the telomere bouquet stage (Martín et 433
- al.,  2014).  However,  it  is  likely  that  not  all  homoeologous  invasions  are  removed,  especially  in 434
- regions  of  extremely  high  similarity  between  homoeologues  ( i.e. at  the  gene  level).  In  these  rare 435
- cases,  the Ph1 locus  acts  also  downstream  by  preventing  the  maturation  of  crossovers  between 436

437

homoeologous chromosomes (Martín et al., 2014). This ultimately contributes to the establishment of

- a diploid-like meiotic behaviour of bread wheat. 438
- Thus,  homoeologous  COs  are  thought  to  arise  predominantly  via  the  class  I  pathway  (ZMM 439
- dependent),  which  exhibits  interference  and  is  more  tolerant  to  sequence  polymorphisms  than  the 440
- class  II  pathway  (MUS81 dependent;  Ziolkowski,  2023).  Indeed,  class  II  crossovers  preferentially 441
- occur in homozygous regions (Blackwell et al., 2020; Crismani et al., 2012; Fernandes et al., 2017), 442
- whereas  class  I  crossovers  can  form  in  polymorphic  regions  and  are  redistributed  according  to 443
- heterozygosity  patterns  (Blackwell  et  al.,  2020;  Ziolkowski  et  al.,  2015).  Consistently,  genes 444
- promoting class I COs  have been directly implicated in the regulation of  homoeologous 445
- recombination as in Brassica napus (Gonzalo et al., 2019; Grandont et al., 2014), supporting the idea 446
- that most homoeologous COs arise through the class I pathway rather than the class II pathway. 447
- Although double mutants do not increase the number of chiasmata, they all exhibit a greater number 448
- of rod bivalents and fewer ring bivalents than the ph1b or zip4-5B single mutants, suggesting a shift 449
- in the  type  of  chromosome associations during meiosis. Loss of ring bivalents for rod bivalents is 450

451

- often associated with problems during synapsis. It has been shown that homoeologous synapsis can

452

- only occur after the telomere bouquet stage has completed, and not during the bouquet stage (Martín
- et  al.,  2017).  Homologous synapsis, in contrast, can occur during the bouquet stage (Martín et al., 453

454

- 2017). The combined loss of ZIP4-5B and MSH7-3D may affect the synapsed homoeologues in some

455

- way at this late stage, resulting in early resolution of homoeologues as rod bivalents rather than ring
- bivalents. It has already been shown that ZIP4-5B is required for completion of synapsis (Draeger et al., 2023).
- Even  though  we  did  not  observe  any  additive  effect  between zip4-5B ( ph1 )  and msh7-3D ( ph2 ) 458

- zip4-5B /CS ph2b and  CS ph1b /Cad msh7-3D .  Moreover,  recombinant  double  mutant  hybrids 474
- (notably Cad zip4-5B /CS ph2b ) exhibited increased meiotic disruptions. This suggests that there are 475
- other  factors  elsewhere  in  the  genome  interacting  with ZIP4-5B and/or MSH7-3D to  control 476
- homoeologous  CO,  and  that  these  interactors  are  more  efficient  when  coming  from  the  same 477
- background.  For  example,  the  MSH7  protein  is  working  as  a  heterodimer  with  MSH2  to  form  a 478
- complex that drives mismatch repair (Culligan and Hays, 2000; Wu et al., 2003).  It is  likely  that 479
- MSH2 and MSH7 could have evolved  concomitantly  in  different  backgrounds  to  retain  maximal 480
- efficiency. Different alleles exist for MSH7-3A and MSH7-3B (Serra et al., 2021), and this could be 481
- the same for MSH2, as well as for other factors that could be interacting with ZIP4-5B and/or MSH7482 3D . 483
- Ph1 has always been assumed to be the main regulator of homoeologous CO in wheat. However, the 484
- homoeologous  copies  of Msh7-3D ( Ph2 )  on  chromosomes  3A  and  3B  may  correspond  to  other 485
- regulators  of  homoeologous  CO  identified previously  (Driscoll,  1972; Mello-Sampayo  and Canas, 486
- 1973; Miller et al., 1983). It is likely that if mutants for two or three copies of MSH7 were to be 487

- (Driscoll, 1972). Also, other genes such as Asy1 (Di Dio et al., 2023) or RECQ4 (Bazile et al., 2024) 491
- seem to affect  homoeologous CO.  It  is  likely  that additional  genes  will  be  discovered  in  the  near 492
- future. 493

4.3 Meiosis is disrupted in ph1 and ph2 mutants 494

- Progression of meiosis was clearly disrupted in all our interspecific hybrids. Similar disruptions have 495
- been  reported  in  previous  studies,  notably  in  interspecific  hybrids  between Triticum  aestivum and 496
- Agropyron  cristatum ,  which  revealed  a  series  of  major  meiotic  disruptions    (Limin  and  Fowler, 497
- 1990).  In  our  material,  we  observed  chromosome fragmentation and bridges, most likely resulting 498
- from homoeologous COs that were not properly resolved during meiosis, due to the absence of the 499
- ZIP4-5B and MSH7-3D proteins. 500
- These abnormalities also  stem  from  complex  interactions  between  genomes  from  distinct  parental 501

502

- species (i.e. wheat and Ae. variabilis ),  which can lead to segregation distortion (Endo, 1990). Such

503

- genomic  interactions can generate various disruptive elements, such as genetic imbalances,
- chromosomal incompatibilities, abnormalities in chiasma formation, divergent hormonal responses, 504
- epigenetic  alterations  and  variations  in  chromosome  number.  Rooted  in  genetic,  structural  and 505
- regulatory  differences  between  parental  species,  these  factors  are  likely  to  compromise  orderly 506
- chromosome segregation and the process of genetic recombination. This highlights the importance of 507
- controlling homologous and homoeologous recombination to prevent loss of fertility in the progeny. 508

5 Conclusion 509

- The production of F1 interspecific hybrids between wheat mutants ( ph1 and/or ph2 )  and Aegilops 510
- variabilis provides  a  valuable  pre-breeding  resource,  as  it  enhances  homoeologous  recombination 511
- and facilitates the introgression of useful traits from Aegilops species that are otherwise difficult to 512
- access through conventional crosses due to meiotic barriers. Following the restoration of fertility in 513
- the  F1  interspecific  hybrid,  successive  backcrosses  with  elite  wheat  are  required  to  stabilize  the 514
- genome and facilitate the evaluation of successfully introgressed Aegilops segments. 515

- Here,  we  demonstrate  that  accumulation  of ph1 ( zip4-5B )  and ph2 ( msh7-3D )  mutations  do  not 516
- enhance homoeologous recombination in comparison with the ph1 alone. Thus, we can confirm that 517
- the ph1 mutation  remains  the  most  efficient  way  to  perform  such  introgressions  within  the  wheat 518
- genome. However, its strong effect is  accompanied by considerable genomic instability,  including 519
- multivalent  formation  and  abnormal  chromosome  segregation,  especially  in  the  deleted  mutants 520
- (Alabdullah et al., 2021; Sánchez-Morán et al., 2001; Sourdille et al., 2025). This instability often 521
- results  in  increased  aneuploidy  and  a  loss  of  fertility,  raising  concerns  about  the  persistence  of 522
- deleterious rearrangements in advanced lines. In contrast, loss of Ph2 function induces a moderate 523
- yet  significant increase  in homoeologous crossovers while it could maintain better overall genome 524
- integrity  and  fertility,  making  it  a  more  practical  option  for  breeders  seeking  to  minimize  post525
- introgression disruptions (Serra et al., 2021). 526
- The choice of genetic background is also a critical factor in wheat pre-breeding programs. Chinese 527
- Spring  is  widely  employed  as  the  reference  background  for  mutations,  but  most  of  the  available 528
- mutants correspond to old deletion lines, and the CS background itself is limited by poor agronomic 529
- performance. The use of the CS background will necessitate that several backcrosses are carried out 530
- to  achieve  agronomic  performance  levels  comparable  to  modern  elite  germplasm  (Türkösi  et  al., 531
- 2022).  In  contrast,  the  Cadenza  background  is  preferred  for  breeding  because  it  exhibits  good 532
- agronomic performance (Ma et al., 2015) and, with EMS-derived point mutants, could result in lower 533
- meiotic instability compared to older CS deletion lines. 534
- Ultimately,  transferring  or  generating Ph mutations  directly  in  elite  wheat  cultivars  could  further 535
- streamline breeding pipelines by reducing the need for extensive backcrossing and accelerating the 536
- release of improved varieties (Li et al., 2020; Türkösi et al., 2022). 537

metadata.preprint_title: Accumulation of ph1 (zip4-5B) and ph2 (msh7-3D) mutations fails to boost homoeologous recombination in hexaploid wheat

pub_metadata.preprint_title: Accumulation of ph1 (zip4-5B) and ph2 (msh7-3D) mutations fails to boost homoeologous recombination in hexaploid wheat
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: HAQUET, C.; NADAUD, I.; MARTIN, A.; REY, M.-D.; BEN BOUSLAH, A.; JOHN-BEJAI, C.; Moore, G.; SOURDILLE, P.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: Diversification of the hexaploid (bread) wheat genetic pool using wild genetic resources relies on effective meiotic recombination (crossover) between wheat chromosomes and their counterparts from related species (homoeologues). However, crossover between homoeologues is normally suppressed by two major genes, ZIP4-5B (Ph1) and MSH7-3D (Ph2). We investigated the effect of introducing zip4-5B and/or msh7-3D mutations into interspecific hybrids derived from crosses between wheat and Aegilops variabilis. Single and double mutants were exploited in Chinese Spring (CS) and Cadenza (Cad) genetic backgrounds, as well as in a CS/Cad recombinant background. The number of univalents, bivalents and multivalents was scored from meiotic cells at metaphase I, from which numbers of chiasmata were deduced. We demonstrated a non-cumulative effect of simultaneous zip4-5B and msh7-3D mutations on homoeologous recombination, as homoeologous crossovers reached a maximum when ZIP4-5B alone was mutated. We also showed that hybrids carrying both the zip4-5B and msh7-3D mutations in the same genetic background exhibited more effective recombination compared to a double mutant in the CS/Cad recombinant background. The progression of meiosis was also monitored in the different interspecific hybrids mutants, revealing clear disruptions. Thus, our study significantly contributes to the optimization of the introgression of beneficial alleles from wild relatives into elite wheat germplasm; first by demonstrating the efficiency of ZIP4-5B and MSH7-3D mutations independently and in combination and second by elucidating the influence of the genetic background in which these mutations are present in an interspecific hybrid context.
